speaker: interviewer
text: OK we were talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lambesc.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lambesc.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lambesc. Where the-- the [? bad ?] sent, it was nice, it was not so hard then. And there where I got in to conflict with the regulations of-- of the camp. And this I said, that there were all kinds of people, homosexuals. And it-- it caused some-- some trouble. And the regulation was, no-- we cannot go promenade on the-- in-- in the-- in the court of the camp. We cannot walk in swim-- in swim trunks. [LAUGHS] Yeah. Because this is what-- what they wanted to-- with no swim trunks. That-- that was too much for me, you know, as an aspirant of the Bill of Rights, it was too much for me. So I just came out in swim trunks. Everybody-- I come in swim-- but the first thing is the-- the-- the-- the sergeant, go and put on your-- your-- your trousers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said no, I won't do it. OK, you go-- you go to the commander. I come to the commander. You will have on-- on the-- the-- in-- in arrest. In the camp, they had in-- a-- a-- a-- a little-- what was it? Where they kept wine, it was a cellar. And there they-- they put me. And they put me. I could have a candle.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could take a-- a-- a pencil with me but no-- no paper to write or something. I could take my French-German dictionary with me. And there I was for 24 hours. Just it was a demonstration. I-- I wrote two nice poems, in the-- in the dictionary I wrote the poems. [INAUDIBLE] saw the poems-- poems and he said that he liked it. Anyway-- What did I want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That. After 24 hours I showed my-- my-- demonstrated my point of view, but I came out. I put on the clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When-- when Hitler came down, then all the camps got the order to go-- to be moved to-- to the French border-- to the Spanish border-- protection, [NON-ENGLISH]. All right. So the whole camp we by-- by-- by train-- by train we put direction to Spanish border. And in [? Poole-- ?] [? Poole ?] is a French thing-- we stopped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was no-- the-- the Germans came down from Belgium, and then they came down along the Spanish border, and they were already there. So there was no way to go to [NON-ENGLISH] to-- to help them. So the train stopped there. And we stayed there maybe a day until the order came back from the French government, go back. So we began going back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I have to say, what happened to the family in the-- in the meantime? The family was in Marseille. The family was bombed there. Before I took off with the train, I heard the bombs in Marseille, and I knew that my family there. And I didn't get for three months no news about the whereabouts of my family. Are they alive? And-- so what-- what did the government do with the-- with the women of the men who are in camp? What did they do? And with the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They also wanted to save them. So they took all the women and the children, you are going to [NON-ENGLISH] too, to the Spanish border. So the family was on the way. Many families were on the way in the train to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am in [? Poole, ?] standing here, yeah? And that train with the family goes here. And my trains starts going to Lourdes, back and stops here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This train here, and this train-- and my train here. And it-- it-- it was early in the morning and these trains stopped. That train cannot move. My train will move. I look out of the window, hallucination. I cannot believe it. There I see my wife with children. Just this-- this wagon stays here and her wagon here. And I-- I couldn't believe it. That was a decision, that is the way that I have to escape, to be with them. First of all, the-- the big relief that they are alive. That I see them. But I couldn't go out. I could only see them. So I knew this train will go back, too. Though my train, in the meantime, had to go, had to went down to the next station. And I was-- I knew that this train will go back too and comes to this. So I went with the train. And there was always a guard in the-- in the wagon, you know. As long as the train is moving, there was no guard. But when the train comes to a stop, there comes this-- comes the guard, and stays at the door so nobody should escape. So I knew, there's only one way, to jump out from the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the train came to the other station and slowed down, I took all my-- my values and threw it out, and jumped after that. There was no guard, but it was already-- the-- the train was slowing down. I could-- they are doing that. And I took my value and there was another car there full of hay. There I-- I was hiding. And hiding and sitting and waiting until her train comes back. But her train was miles long with soldiers. The-- the-- the-- the state-- the state broke down, you know? Soldiers on flight, peasants on flight, all-- refugees, where the Germans came and they ran away. And it was impossible to enter this train, it was so full with-- with people and these families.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have to go. I have to go to-- to find then. So I went-- her train came to a stop in this station. I entered the train, and now I had to go. Took me hours and hours and hours to walk through the-- I started from the-- from the beginning, because I didn't know where-- where her-- and it tooks hours. Oh, but you want we had another honeymoon, dear, when we met. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the train went-- then the train went-- where did the train go? Oh,

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- then I was with her. I escaped. I escaped. I was with her, and the train went back, back to Marseille. So-- but my train with my camp, they went in the camp to [NON-ENGLISH]. And that, I tell you, that was my-- my luck. The first luck was that I escaped and avoided to go to [NON-ENGLISH], where the most of the people were captured by the Germans and took to the ovens.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back to Marseille, she with her papers from the government. Still she was under the-- under the-- the-- under the guard of the government. And I had no papers. Everybody could-- if a police asked me, who are you? I would be arrested. So she went back in the same hotel which the [NON-ENGLISH] provided for her before, and-- but there was no [NON-ENGLISH] anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were running away too. So there-- we had nothing to eat. We had absolutely no nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where to eat? There was a-- a Catholic mission. The nuns, they gave us food. They gave us food. It's interesting. It-- it was an old monastery with heavy, heavy tables. No dishes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had, like in the Medieval time, carved out in the-- in the-- in the heavy tables, in the-- in the-- in the-- yeah, table, carved out, and they poured the soup in the table. From there we ate. And was quite an-- it was the old Medieval time that we-- Anyway, then there were [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] spiritualists. Spiritualists. I don't know. We came there to eat. Tables with linen and nice silver and porcelain.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, we got excellent food. Where did they get this soup? Spiritualists, you know? They-- they were believing in ghosts. Did the ghosts pay for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway-- but that-- that also, we run-- we run out of that. We couldn't go all the time. And we get all wine. Anyway. So, we thought, we cannot support the children, what should we do? So we went to the Quakers. The Quakers, they took care, still they had a-- a big, big-- a castle, they had, where they took the children from Spanish war, from 1934, the Franco refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Franco refugees' children they took to the castle and they fed them. So our children went there, and we had them there. It was-- we-- we could survive, my wife and I, because-- for-- for the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the Quakers took care. The Quakers. It developed that we-- a-- a friendship developed between the head of the Quakers, Kirschner. Mrs. and Mr. Kirschner. They were very fine people. I have a book by him. It-- and-- yeah. So, then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we had the children there. And-- no, wait a minute. Oh, no, no, no, no, no. No, no, no, no. When I-- when we came to-- when-- I was ahead of time. When we came to Marseille, so I was-- I was free, but I had no papers. But the family and now came the order that these families all-- they have to go in the camp in Marseille. [NON-ENGLISH]. Camp [INAUDIBLE]. And I took them there, and I put them in-- in to-- into prison. I was cold but I-- I was free. Weeks later, maybe two weeks later, I heard the rumor that the commander of that camp is this SOB. He steals the money. He doesn't feed the children. The children are sick. The children are hungry. I was desperate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I made a bundle. I made a bundle with food. And it was stupid. And I go there and tell the soldier, please give it to Madame Buch. He, all right, but I cannot do it. I have to-- to have the permission from the Lieutenant. Comes the Lieutenant. All right, I will do it, but the commander has to know it. The Commander invites me to, wants to-- to see me. And I-- I was stupid, you know? He says, if-- if your family is in camp, you're supposed to be in camp too. How come that you're walking around? So, in that moment he says to the guard, get out of the room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, if you are free, you are an escapee, and you-- you can tell me. That what happened to you will happen to me in three months. I am Jewish. I am a French officer, but that what will happen to me. I give you the family. You can have the family. But you have to have papers. And he gives me a note to-- to a Capuchin monk-- to a Capuchin monk. I have-- I have his name. And I have also an article about him in the 'Congressional Record.' You will go in the-- close to where he is. He might-- you might meet him in-- in-- in a nun's habit. He is in the French underground. [? Communist. ?] He is going to provide paper for you. As I write in the 'Congressional Record,' he-- he made stamps, false documents. He made. But he didn't-- he didn't give me a false document. He said-- he said to me, you have a-- I have a friend at-- at the city hall of Marseille. All you need, you need papers. And that papers will-- when you go with the papers, you will have your family. So I went to the city hall, got the paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And went to the camp where my family is. Wanted to talk to the-- to the commander, which I thought is an SOB. He doesn't want to see me. That man had style, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew that I am going to kiss his-- his shoes. So and he gave me the family. He-- he-- I didn't see him. He gave me the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I tell you what I found out later on from a-- from a man-- from a young-- from a kid? He was at that-- that time 10 years old when he was also with his mother there. I met him in Long Beach by accident, and he remembered-- he remembered this [NON-ENGLISH], and so on. I was-- I was in prison with his father in [NON-ENGLISH]. So he said that that commander of the camp was really a thief. He was an-- he was an Algerian-- he was an Algerian officer. And just two days he went on vacation. Always he was there. Only for two days there was a Jewish commander there. You see? And God told me, you go there with the packet. Just on this day, you go there, when the other son of a bitch is not there. You will find it. What a luck, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here you have the train, God's finger. Here you have this accident that I just go on the-- on a day when the-- when there is a human being there who has pity with me and gives me back the family. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the-- now I-- I have paper. I have the family. But what shall I do with the kids? I give them to the Quakers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife and I, we have a good time. A good time, you can imagine a good time. Because there was-- there was-- the [NON-ENGLISH] was gone. [NON-ENGLISH] was gone. There was only the-- the Quakers but the Quakers were out, far away from us in the-- in the camp. So we had-- didn't have-- But in the meantime comes the-- comes the visa. The visa we have. And which I-- from the camp, you know, from the prison camp, I was allowed, under guard-- I had always a guard, a military guard with me-- to visit the consulate for-- for the visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got-- we got the visa. Now we-- we are going to go to Bordeaux, and take a boat and go to the America. And we want to go to Bordeaux.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bordeaux is closed. No boats anymore. You have your visa, but you cannot go out. We had to wait one year until we had a chance to-- to go to-- over Spain to Portugal to take a boat in-- in-- from Lisbon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to stay in Barcelona and wait, all-- all with the help of the-- of the [NON-ENGLISH]. All your money, you know? All the money you-- you-- you gave them. And we got a ticket to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So here we are. We're in the United States. Before we get in-- into that, I just want to ask you, were you all this time, in touch with your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in touch with your mother and father back in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. My mother, fortunately, she died in '33.

speaker: interviewer
text: Natural causes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Natural causes. Oh. My father-- my father, he-- he was in-- in pension. Pension. And he-- he lived in Berlin. And as I said, Berlin was a very nationalist city. Nazis. It was the headquarter of the Nazis. Oh, my brother, my brother was a physician in-- in Berlin. He had to close too. He-- he went to America. You know, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- tell me about your father. Did your father survive? What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- I want to talk about my brother, then. Maybe first-- first my brother. My brother, he had to close his-- his office. He was a physician. And-- but-- yeah. That was still at the time when I was in Ankara. And he asked me to help him financially. And I could. Yeah. I took a-- a good-- a good part of my savings and sent it to him so he can go out. It happened a few-- a few-- maybe a half year, it happened to me. Ah, yeah. And he came to America. And had to-- to-- to study. He had to study medicine, American medicine, for one year, and became the staff physician in Kankakee in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Illinois.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know Kankakee. Yeah. And-- yeah, yeah. At that time, they didn't know what cigarettes are doing. He was a heavy smoker. And he was healthy, and he could be alive today, but he was a heavy smoker. Emphysema, heart attack, and died at 69. OK. That was the brother. He-- he could save his life.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, yeah. They didn't touch him. Only they came once and asked, where is Fred Buch? Where is your son? Because I told you in the-- the-- that I-- I was the-- the-- the founder of the-- the Free-- Free Organization of Jewish students.

speaker: interviewee
text: Free means whether you are a socialist, whether you are a religious man, whether you are [INAUDIBLE], we are all Jewish students. We are national students. We are not nationalists, but we know our enemy. And we know-- so-- and we were quite strong. And we-- we fought the Nazis wherever we could, when-- even when we had to have arms with us. We-- we never used them, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they were looking for me. Yeah, and my father, he had his pension. Then came the order, he cannot stay anymore in Berlin, because it is too-- cleansing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The city shall be cleaned of Jews. You have to Vienna. The old man had to go and give up his-- his home, and rents-- rents a-- a-- a room in-- in Vienna, hard to find. And here comes again when-- when I said he has to go out, he needs a-- he needs a visa. And I was already in America. He needs a visa. I didn't have-- I was not citizen, even. I cannot send him an affidavit. I didn't earn enough money. I didn't have-- so I said the rich-- his cousin, the rich aunt who-- who didn't want any-- any-- didn't want to give me another affidavit, besides when we arrived. She invited us to come, and she gave every kid two oranges and a t-shirt. That was her gift to them.
